Details: Oxidation of dichlorvos by ozone can be effective depending on the ozone dose, contact time and water quality parameters. One study showed complete oxidation of dichlorvos in 20 minutes with ozone dose of 1 mg/L; the time required for complete removal decreased with increased dose. The pH, however, was 4. Another study at pH 7.3 showed that oxidation of dichlorvos increased with increased ozone dose from 1 mg/L to 5 mg/L; control ranged from 17 to 73 percent, respectively. A third study at pH 8 showed greater than 80 percent oxidation. No studies reported transformation products. One study looked at catalytic ozonation using microporous silicate column showed no dichlorvos removal after two hours of ozonation.
